name: Sierra Crestellina Refuge Trail
name_original: Puerto de las Viñas – trail run circular desde Casares por la Sierra Crestellina
description: Challenge yourself on the difficult 5.5-mile Sierra Crestellina Refuge Trail, offering panoramic views and a chance to spot griffon vultures
region_intro: Jogging routes around Casares traverse a diverse landscape, characterized by the foothills of the Sierra Bermeja mountains and the rugged terrain of the Sierra Crestellina Natural Park. The region features lush green valleys, hills adorned with olive groves, and whitewashed villages. While primarily inland, some routes offer proximity to the Costa del Sol's coastline. This varied topography provides a range of running experiences for different fitness levels. Best jogging routes around Casares The most popular jogging route is Sierra Crestellina Refuge Trail, a 5.5 miles (8.8 km) trail that takes 1 hour 24 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers panoramic views and opportunities to spot griffon vultures. Another top favourite among local runners is La Duquesa Marina – Castillo de la Duquesa loop from La Perla de Bahía, a moderate 5.6 miles (9.0 km) path. This route explores coastal areas, including a marina and a historic castle. Local runners also love the Cubanita loop from San Luis de Sabinillas, a 3.2 miles (5.1 km) trail leading through coastal surroundings, often completed in about 55 minutes. Jogging around Casares is defined by mountain foothills, green valleys, and coastal paths. The network of running routes offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to more challenging mountain trails. The routes in Casares are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.1 stars from over 25 reviews. More than 2,600 runners have used komoot to explore Casares's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Casares?
A: Casares offers a diverse selection of running routes. There are over 10 dedicated jogging routes, ranging from moderate to difficult, providing options for various fitness levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on running trails around Casares?
A: The running trails around Casares feature a diverse landscape. You'll find routes traversing the foothills of the &lt;strong&gt;Sierra Bermeja mountains&lt;/strong&gt; and the rugged terrain of the &lt;strong&gt;Sierra Crestellina Natural Park&lt;/strong&gt;. Expect lush green valleys, hills adorned with olive groves, and some routes offering proximity to the Costa del Sol's coastline. The village itself also offers unique urban jogging through its narrow, cobblestoned streets.

Q: Are there any running routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easier jog?
A: Yes, there are moderate options available. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10282302" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Duquesa Marina – Castillo de la Duquesa loop from La Perla de Bahía&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 9 km path exploring coastal areas. Another moderate choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40391560" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cubanita loop from San Luis de Sabinillas&lt;/a&gt;, a 5.1 km trail through coastal surroundings.

Q: Can I find challenging running routes with significant elevation gain in Casares?
A: Absolutely. Casares is situated amidst mountains, offering routes with substantial elevation. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996240362/sierra-crestellina-refuge-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra Crestellina Refuge Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 8.8 km route with over 330 meters of elevation gain, offering panoramic views. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/21824236" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Casares – View of Casares loop from Casares&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 19.7 km route with nearly 800 meters of ascent.

Q: Are there any running routes that offer great views or pass by interesting landmarks?
A: Many routes in Casares provide stunning vistas. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996240362/sierra-crestellina-refuge-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra Crestellina Refuge Trail&lt;/a&gt; offers panoramic views of the Sierra Crestellina Natural Park. You can also find routes that lead to viewpoints like the &lt;strong&gt;Mirador del Tajo de la Planá&lt;/strong&gt; or the &lt;strong&gt;Mirador de Casares&lt;/strong&gt;, providing breathtaking views of the white village, mountains, and the distant sea. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10282302" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Duquesa Marina – Castillo de la Duquesa loop&lt;/a&gt; explores coastal areas and passes by the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5371075" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de la Duquesa&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What natural features can I expect to see while jogging in Casares?
A: Casares is rich in natural beauty. You'll encounter the rugged landscapes of the &lt;strong&gt;Sierra Crestellina Natural Park&lt;/strong&gt;, known for its cork oak and Mediterranean forests. The area also features lush green valleys, olive groves, and the dramatic &lt;strong&gt;Tajo de la Virgen Gorge&lt;/strong&gt;. From higher vantage points, you might even spot griffon vultures soaring overhead, as the Sierra Crestellina is a significant nesting site.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in the Casares area?
A: Yes, several routes are designed as loops. Examples include the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10282302" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Duquesa Marina – Castillo de la Duquesa loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40391560" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cubanita loop from San Luis de Sabinillas&lt;/a&gt;. For a more challenging experience, consider the difficult &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/21824236" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Casares – View of Casares loop from Casares&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year to go jogging in Casares?
A: Casares is an excellent destination for outdoor activities year-round. However, for comfortable jogging, spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant landscapes. Summers can be hot, so early morning or late evening runs are advisable. Winters are generally mild, making it possible to jog comfortably, especially on sunny days.

Q: Are there parking facilities available near the running trailheads?
A: While specific parking details for every trailhead are not provided, Casares is a popular destination, and parking is generally available in and around the village, as well as near popular coastal starting points like La Duquesa Marina. It's advisable to check local signage for parking regulations.

Q: What do other runners say about their experience jogging in Casares?
A: The running routes in Casares are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.1 stars from over 25 reviews. Runners often praise the varied terrain, the stunning panoramic views, and the opportunities to explore both mountain and coastal landscapes.

Q: Are there any historical sites or attractions I can visit near the running paths?
A: Yes, Casares is rich in history and attractions. The ruins of the 13th-century Moorish &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5571188" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Eagle Castle&lt;/a&gt;, perched at the top of the village, offer incredible panoramic views. The ancient Roman sulfur-rich thermal baths, &lt;strong&gt;Baños de la Hedionda&lt;/strong&gt;, are also nearby. Along coastal routes, you might encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5371075" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de la Duquesa&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Where can I find refreshments or places to relax after a run in Casares?
A: The charming white village of Casares itself offers a selection of cafes and restaurants where you can enjoy refreshments and local cuisine. If your run takes you towards the coast, areas like La Duquesa Marina also provide various dining options and places to relax by the sea.
tips: The port overlooks Sierra Crestellina, named after the long ridge that crosses the top of the rock, it is a protected natural area of the Serranía de Ronda. The highest point is located in the Cerro de las Chapas, at 946 meters high.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for jogging on this route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is quite challenging, featuring uneven surfaces with steep ascents and descents, especially around the Refuge itself. You'll encounter rocky sections and limestone slabs, which can be slippery when wet. While some parts may be well-surfaced, others are less maintained, requiring careful footing.

faqs.question: What kind of views and natural features can I expect?
faqs.answer: This route offers magnificent panoramic views of the Sierra Crestellina mountains, Monte del Duque, and the picturesque white village of Casares. On clear days, you can even see the Rock of Gibraltar and the distant African coast. The landscape is characterized by rugged limestone formations and typical Mediterranean vegetation, including holm oaks, pines, and carob trees.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginner joggers?
faqs.answer: No, this trail is generally considered difficult for jogging. It requires good fitness, sure-footedness, and sturdy shoes due to its steep, rocky sections and uneven paths. It's best suited for experienced joggers looking for a challenging experience.

faqs.question: What wildlife might I see along the way?
faqs.answer: The Sierra Crestellina is famous for its significant population of griffon vultures. You'll have a unique opportunity to observe these majestic birds soaring overhead, particularly from viewpoints like Los Buitres. The area's Mediterranean vegetation also supports various other bird species and small mammals.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to jog this route is typically during the cooler months, from autumn through spring (October to May). Summers can be very hot, making the strenuous ascents and descents more challenging. Spring offers pleasant temperatures and vibrant flora, while autumn provides comfortable conditions.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the Sierra Crestellina Refuge Trail?
faqs.answer: Generally, dogs are permitted on trails within natural parks in Spain, but they should always be kept on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure the safety of other trail users. Please ensure you carry enough water for your dog, especially given the challenging terrain and potential for high temperatures.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or is there an entrance fee for the Paraje Natural Sierra Crestellina?
faqs.answer: As of current information, there is no specific entrance fee or permit required to access the trails within the Paraje Natural Sierra Crestellina for general recreational use like jogging. However, always check local regulations before your visit, as rules can change. Respect the natural environment and follow any posted guidelines.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trailhead?
faqs.answer: This circular route typically starts from Casares. While specific parking details for the exact trailhead aren't provided, you can usually find parking options within or on the outskirts of Casares, which serves as a common starting point for trails in the Sierra Crestellina area. It's advisable to arrive early, especially on weekends, to secure a spot.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, besides the stunning panoramic views, the route passes by the 18th-century Fountain of La Arquita. You'll also traverse the Puerto de las Viñas, a mountain pass that offers great vistas.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, while primarily following the Refugio Sierra Crestellina trail (98% of the route), it also briefly intersects with sections of other paths. You'll find it crosses parts of the Genalguacil - Casares trail and the Camino de Gaucin, among others.

faqs.question: What should I wear and bring for jogging on this trail?
faqs.answer: Given the difficult terrain, sturdy jogging shoes with good grip are essential. We recommend wearing layers, as temperatures can vary, and bringing plenty of water, especially since water sources on the trail are limited. Sun protection (hat, sunscreen) is also advisable, along with a map or GPS device, as some paths can be less maintained.
